coredata.dc:title: Enhancement of lignin-based carbon quantum dots from poplar pre-hydrolysis liquor on photocatalytic CO2 reduction via TiO2 nanosheets
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Qichao
Yuan, Zhimin
Yang, Guihua
Tian, Zhongjian
Jiang, Zaiyong
Zhang, Kai
Wang, Chao
Chen, Jiachuan
coredata.dc:description: Reasonable utilization of lignin in pre-hydrolysis liquor (PHL) is very important for the economic benefit of papermaking industry. In this paper, lignin in poplar pre-hydrolysis liquor (PHL) was effectively adsorbed by TiO2 nanosheets, realizing the purification of PHL. Subsequently, lignin was carbonized via thermal desorption for forming the carbon quantum dots immobilized on TiO2 nanosheets surface, which can improve both the separation efficiency of photogenerated carrier and CO2 adsorption capacity of TiO2, as a result the efficiency of photocatalytic CO2 reduction of C/TiO2 increased 300 % compared to the TiO2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pre-hydrolysis liquor
Lignin
TiO2
Carbon quantum dots
Photocatalytic CO2 reduction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of C/TiO2-X preparation.
a) XRD spectra of as-synthesized samples. b) Raman spectra of the photocatalysts.
SEM patterns of the samples: (a) C/TiO2-1、(b) C/TiO2-2、(c) C/TiO2-3、(d) C/TiO2-4.
TEM images of the samples: (a, b) pristine TiO2, (c, d) C/TiO2-3.
a) UV–vis absorption spectra of as-prepared samples. b) PL spectra of pristine the photocatalysts.
CO2 adsorption isotherms of TiO2, and C/TiO2-3 samples at 298 K.
a) EIS and b) photocurrent intensity of pristine TiO2 and C/TiO2-3; c) CO yield of all samples after 4 h UV–vis light irradiation. d) Repeated activity of C/TiO2-3 sample.
Schematic diagram of the photocatalytic CO2 reduction with C/TiO2-X.
Main chemical compositions of the PHL.

head.ce:title: Enhancement of lignin-based carbon quantum dots from poplar pre-hydrolysis liquor on photocatalytic CO2 reduction via TiO2 nanosheets

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bio-based Materials and Green Papermaking /Key Lab of Pulp & Paper Science and Technology of Education Ministry of China, Qilu University of Technology, Ji’nan, Shandong, 250353, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Bio-based Materials and Green Papermaking /Key Lab of Pulp & Paper Science and Technology of Education Ministry of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bio-based Materials and Green Papermaking /Key Lab of Pulp & Paper Science and Technology of Education Ministry of China, Qilu University of Technology, Ji’nan, Shandong 250353, P.R.China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Qichao Gao and Zhimin Yuan contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reasonable utilization of lignin in pre-hydrolysis liquor (PHL) is very important for the economic benefit of papermaking industry. In this paper, lignin in poplar pre-hydrolysis liquor (PHL) was effectively adsorbed by TiO2 nanosheets, realizing the purification of PHL. Subsequently, lignin was carbonized via thermal desorption for forming the carbon quantum dots immobilized on TiO2 nanosheets surface, which can improve both the separation efficiency of photogenerated carrier and CO2 adsorption capacity of TiO2, as a result the efficiency of photocatalytic CO2 reduction of C/TiO2 increased 300 % compared to the TiO2.

ce:section.ce:para: In the process of dissolving pulp production, some hemicellulose and lignin in wood chips need to be dissolved and removed via hot water pretreatment, resulting in pre-hydrolyzed liquid (PHL). (Chirat et al., 2012; Huijgen et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2019; Soudham et al., 2011; Hendriks and Zeeman, 2009). In order to realize the high value application of hemicellulose, lignin must be removed from PHL (Wen et al., 2013; Martinez et al., 2000; Zhang et al., 2018; Martinez et al., 2001; Zhu et al., 2015). Moreover, according to statistics, the annual global lignin production is about 70 million tons, of which only about 5% is effectively used, and the remaining about 95 % is carbonized to produce heat or discharged with black liquor (Lievonen et al., 2015; Laurichesse and Averous, 2014). If such a large amount of lignin is used reasonably, it will be of great scientific significance.

Currently, some high value utilization of lignin has been reported. For example, Yang et al. (2015) prepared nanocomposite membrane materials by using lignin nanoparticles, which improved the mechanical properties and ultraviolet absorption capacity of the membrane. Wu et al. (2012) synthetized expandable phenolic resin using phenolized lignin, which exhibited high thermal stability and flame retardancy due to the lignin participated in phenolization and resin curing reactions. Han et al. (2016) obtained lignin-based activated carbon via activating nitrate ethanol lignin via water vapor, which enhanced adsorption of nitrophenol and Congo red. Fu et al. (2020) prepared a porous carbon material from lignin to assemble supercapacitors, which exhibited high circulation and stability. Nelson et al. (2016) used chestnut tannin to prepare a kind of carbon material, which had 3.44 mmol/g capacity of CO2 adsorption at 0 °C and 1 atm. Among previous studies, the exploration on lignin-based carbon has attracted the researchers' much attention.

Hence, a novel approach for the reasonable utilization of lignin in PHL was explored: Firstly, adsorbing the lignin on the surface of semiconductor photocatalysts, subsequently transforming the adsorbed lignin into carbon quantum dots (C dots). During the process of transformation, C dots were firmly immobilized on TiO2 nanosheet surface and formed composites labelled as C/TiO2-X (X = 1,2,3,4) varied from lignin content. The carbon quantum dots derived from lignin significantly improved the TiO2 property for photocatalytic CO2 reduction. A new approach for the reasonable utilization of lignin in pre-hydrolysis liquor was investigated in this research.

Poplar wood chips were taken from a papermaking factory located in the east of China. Poplar chips for experiment were first screened and washed, and then kept in a polypropylene ziplock bag to balance moisture for use. Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China) provided the commercial 40 % hydrofluoric acid (HF) solution and tetrabutyl titanate. All experimental chemicals fall into the analytical class.

The preparation of PHL refers to the previously reported method. (Leschinnsky et al., 2009; Shi et al., 2011; Shen et al., 2011; Li et al., 2019). The poplar wood chips were pretreated in a horizontal temperature-controlled digester (No. 2611, KRK company, Tokyo, Japan). The poplar wood chips were mixed with deionized water at 1:6 ratio of solid to liquid (w/v). The pretreatment conditions were 170 °C and retention 60 min with the heating rate approximately 2.5 °C/min. After pretreatment, cool the digester to room temperature by cold water. Then filtration with a 200-mesh slurry bag, the pre-hydrolysate was stored in refrigerator at 4 °C before later use or analysis.

The synthesizing process of TiO2 nanosheets was that 3 mL of HF solution and 25 mL of tetrabutyl titanate were well mixed by stirring for 30 min (Yu et al., 2014). The mixture was taken out from the autoclave, and then synthesized at 200 °C for 24 h in an oven. After cooling down, the TiO2 sample was removed from autoclave, filtered and washed with water and ethanol in squence, and then dried at 60 °C before being used for the experiments.

The preparation process of composite catalyst of C/TiO2 indicated below: 1 mL, 2 mL, 3 mL and 4 mL of hydrolysate were respectively diluted to 20 mL, then mixed with 0.3 g of TiO2 under vigorous stirring for 2 h. The mixture samples were filtered and placed in tube furnace and calcined with argon gas at 500 °C for 3 h. Subsequently, the samples were collected and marked as C/TiO2-1, C/TiO2-2, C/TiO2-3, and C/TiO2-4 sample based on hydrolysate dosage for analysis and detection in subsequent experiments.

The sugars in PHL were determinated with an ion chromatography (Dionex ICS-5000+ DC, Thermo Fisher Scientific, MA, USA) with a CarboPac PA10 column, EC detector. 5-Hydroxymethyl furfural (5-HMF), furfural and acetic acid in PHL were analyzed using HPLC (LC-20CE, Shimadzu Corp., Tokyo, Japan) with a Diamonsil C18 (2) column, UV–vis detector (Snelders et al., 2014). The BET surface areas of the samples were determined by a Kubo ×1000 analyzer. FTIR with a spectrometer (ALPHA-T, Bruker, Germany) was employed for analyzing the groups of lignin or TiO2 samples. The element contents of samples were evaluated via the element analyzer (Vario EL, Elementar, Germany). The samples crystallinity was analyzed by an X-ray diffraction (SmartLabSE, Rigaku, Japan) equipped with Cu KαX-ray radiation. Raman spectra of samples were recorded on a laser confocal Raman Spectrometer (inVia, RENISHAW, England) under the wavelength of 532 nm. The surface morphologies of samples were analyzed by SEM (Regulus 8220, Hitachi, Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 5.0 kV. The morphology of samples was obtained by TEM (JEM 2100, Japan) with a copper network for placing samples. To analyze the optical performance, the ultraviolet absorbability of all samples was detected by a UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum (DRS) (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan), and a BaSO4 integrating sphere was used as a reference. The CO2 adsorption and desorption measurements of samples by using a Kubo ×1000 analyzer under high purity CO2 gas environment. The spectra of phot-luminescence (PL) recorded via a fluorospectro photometer (F-7100, Hitachi, Japan) under the excitation wavelength of 320 nm.

The photoconversion process of CO2 was examined under the irradiation of ultraviolet-visible light (UV–vis) from a 300 W PLT-SXE300 xenon lamp. Firstly, 0.1 g sample and 100 mL H2O were mixed and stirred for 15 min in a quartz reactor. Then, the gaseous CO2 (99.99 %) was pumped into the quartz reactor for 15 min at temperature of 15 °C. Afterwards, sealed the reactor and switched on the xenon lamp. Within set period of time, the gas was sampled with a microsampler, and then monitored by a gaschromatograph (GC-7920, Beijing, China) carried with a FID detector and Propark Q column.

Pre-hydrolysis is a separated process of hemicellulose and lignin from the raw materials through a hydrothermal pretreatment method (Guo et al., 2020). PHL was collected from the liquid after pre-hydrolysis. The dissolved organic component of the PHL were shown in Table 1 . Xylose and xylan were the main sugars in PHL, which derives from the degradation of hemicellulose, and their concentration were 2.98 g/L and 10.23 g/L, respectively. In addition, lignin, as the main by-product of the pre- hydrolysis process, had a concentration 5.27 g/L in the PHL, which was not conducive to the separation of sugars. Moreover, the concentration of acetic acid was 2.55 g/L, which derived from the removal of acetyl groups on the side chains from the hemicellulose (Zhang et al., 2010). Furfural (0.99 g/L) and 5-HMF (0.06 g/L) were mainly attributed to the degradation of pentose and hexose (Yemis and Mazza, 2011).

The scheme of the synthesis process for lignin carbon quantum dots was shown in Fig. 1 . We verified that part of the dissolved lignin in the PHL was adsorbed on the surface of TiO2 in the process: about 19.50 % of the dissolved lignin and 2.80 % of the total dissolved xylose and xylan were adsorbed onto the TiO2 surface. Fig. 1 indicated that the C/TiO2-X samples possessed a darker color compared to pristine TiO2. Subsequently, Fig. S1 provides the N2-adsorption isotherm of C/TiO2-3 and pristine TiO2. The surface area of original TiO2 and C/TiO2-3 were 50.49 m2/g and 21.89 m2/g, respectively. Typical functional groups of these two samples (C/TiO2-3 and original TiO2) were detected by FTIR (Fig. S2). It was clearly observed that the unique Ti-O-Ti stretching vibrations of TiO2 ranged from 700 cm−1 to 500 cm−1 (Sarteep et al., 2016; Bae and Choi, 2003). The stretching vibrations at 3426 and 1625 cm−1 assigned to OH bond. While the peaks observed at 1331 cm−1 showed the C H or CO bond derived from syringyl lignin patterns (Zhou et al., 2020). It may be concluded that lignin in PHL was absorbed on TiO2 surface. The high temperature treatment resulted in a lower element carbon content of 0.99 %, which was due to the degradation of lignin to generate gas or volatile products during the high-temperature pyrolysis process (Brebu and Vasile, 2010; Faravelli et al., 2010).

Fig. 2 shows the crystalline structure of samples analyzed by XRD and Raman spectra. Fig. 2a provides the XRD image of C/TiO2 composite and original TiO2, and characteristic diffraction peaks in XRD patterns accorded with anatase titanium dioxide (JCPDS No. 21-1272), which implying that the diffraction peaks of C-TiO2 samples have no obvious changes in terms of primary titanium dioxide. Fig. 2b indicated that the unique anatase Raman bands were presented in original TiO2 and C/TiO2 Raman spectra. The peaks in various modes of the anatase phase of TiO2 were given as follows: 142 cm−1 corresponding to Eg, 195 cm−1 corresponding to Eg, 395 cm−1 corresponding to B1g, 513 cm−1 corresponding to A1g and 639 cm−1 corresponding to Eg (Jiang et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2011; Pan et al., 2013; Jiang et al., 2017). The above results indicated the introduction of carbon quantum dots has no influence on the crystalline structures of TiO2.

Subsequently, the morphologies of the as-prepared samples were investigated via TEM and SEM. Fig. SEM images of C/TiO2-X samples are demostrated in 3a-d. No clear difference was observed among Fig. 3 a, b, c and d, which indicated C/TiO2 composite maintained the morphology of primary TiO2 nanosheet. In addition, Fig. 4 provides the TEM pictures of pristine TiO2 and C/TiO2-3. As described in Fig. 4, the sizes of pristine TiO2 and C/TiO2-3 sample have no obvious change. Fig. 4a exhibits the length and width of TiO2 nanosheets were 100 nm and 50 nm, respectively. Fig. 4b indicates that the lattice spacing of the nanosheet is ca. 0.349 nm, agreeing with the 101 plane d-spacing of anatase titanium dioxide (0.351 nm). As can be seen from Fig. 4c-d, many C dots were uniformly distributed on TiO2 surface with a size of about 3 nm. Fig. S3 shows the results obtained from the TEM images of the C/TiO2-3 sample after three photocatalytic cycles. It was clearly observed that the lignin-based carbon quantum dots were still uniformly fixed on the TiO2 nanosheets, indicating C/TiO2-3 photocatalysts were very stable.

Correspondingly, the optical properties of samples were analyzed via UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig.5 a). Compared to original TiO2, the C/TiO2-X samples show obvious absorbance red shift, indicating that the introduction of lignin-derived carbon quantum dots has leaded to the variation of TiO2 absorption behavior. PL spectra was used to investigate photo-generated holes and electrons recombination rate, and the result was exhibited in Fig. 5b. As shown in this PL spectrum, the main emission peak position of C/TiO2-3 is located at about 403 nm, which is similar with the pristine TiO2 sample. In addition, It can be seen that the emission intensity of C/TiO2-3 decreased, exhibiting a lower recombination rate than pristine TiO2 sample. This result implies that C/TiO2-X can exhibit better photocatalytic activity compared to pristine TiO2.

The CO2 adsorption isotherms of pristine TiO2 and C/TiO2-3 were performed under ambient conditions (298 K) and the analyzing results were provided in Fig. 6 . Fig. 6 indicates that the CO2 adsorption capacities of C/TiO2-3 were higher than pristine TiO2. Among them, C/TiO2-3 capacity was about 1.4 times as much as the original TiO2, which showed best performance. Conclusively, lignin-based carbon quantum dots can improve TiO2 capacity of CO2 adsorption.

The conductivities of C/TiO2-X composite were analyzed by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As shown in Fig. 7 a, this result suggests that the radius of the C/TiO2-3 sample was much smaller than the pristine TiO2 sample, which is beneficial to photocatalytic activity. The photocurrent of the C/TiO2-3 and pristine TiO2 samples were tested in a 0.2 M Na2SO4 solution by using a 300 W xenon lamp and shown in Fig. 7b. As can be seen the photocurrent intensity of the pristine TiO2 was much lower than C/TiO2-3, indicating the C/TiO2-3 sample exhibited higher carrier separation efficiency. Fig. 7c shows the C/TiO2-X activities of photocatalytic CO2 reduction under UV–vis light irradiation. Primary TiO2 was also tested under same conditions for comparing. It was observed that C/TiO2-3 sample exhibited much better activity of CO2 reduction than TiO2. After 4 h of irradiation, the CO2 reduction activity of the C/TiO2-3 sample was about three times compared to primary TiO2. In addition, we have performed cyclic photocatalytic CO2 reduction experiments of the C/TiO2-3 sample to verify the recyclability and stability of composite. The result was shown in Fig. 7d. It can be seen that C/TiO2-3 sample exhibited relatively stable cyclic performance.

According to the above experimental results, we proposed a possible reaction mechanism of C/TiO2-3 composites to enhance the photocatalytic CO2 reaction, which were shown in Fig. 8 . TiO2 generates electrons and holes under the UV–vis irradiation, and then the e-flowed to the surface of lignin-based carbon quantum, thereby reducing the CO2 to CO gas. The lignin-based carbon quantum dots made the photogenerated electrons and holes realize the effective spatial separation, and the left h + oxidized the H2O to produce O2. In addition, it improved the CO2 adsorption capacity, obtaining more CO2 molecule to be transferred into CO gas.

The reasonable utilization of lignin in poplar pre-hydrolysis liquor (PHL) derived from pulp and paper industry was achieved via combining the biomass with semiconductor photocatalysts. The result indicated that 19.50 % of the dissolved lignin and 2.80 % of the total dissolved xylose and xylan were adsorbed onto the TiO2 surface. Lignin of PHL was transferred into carbon quantum dots, immobilized on TiO2 nanosheets, which improved both the separation efficiency of photogenerated carrier and CO2 adsorption capacity of TiO2 nanosheets. The lignin carbon quantum dots immobilized on TiO2 nanosheets increased the efficiency of photocatalytic CO2 reduction of the TiO2 nanosheets by 300 %.

Qichao Gao: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Zhimin Yuan: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Guihua Yang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Zhongjian Tian: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology. Zaiyong Jiang: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology. Kai Zhang: Validation, Visualization. Chao Wang: Validation, Visualization. Jiachuan Chen: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 31670595, 31770628), the Taishan Scholars Program, the Shandong Province Natural Science Foundation (ZR2018BB042), and Shandong Province Key Research and Development Projects (Grant No. 2019JZZY010326, 2019JZZY010328), and Pilot Project for Integrating Science, Education and Industry (2020KJC-ZD14).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.indcrop.2020.113161.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
